**Irror Group**  
CMSC 126

**Introduction**

[Tacfinity](http://tacfinity.vercel.app/) is a customizable offline Tic Tac Toe game originally built by one of the members as a personal project to practice implementing minimax and alpha-beta pruning. The game supports any grid size up to 20×20 with configurable win conditions and multiple AI difficulty levels. [Tacfinity github repo](https://github.com/crammiee/Tacfinity)

We are now scaling it from a local-only game into a full online platform. Think [chess.com](http://chess.com) but for Tic Tac Toe. Features include matchmaking, custom rooms, accounts, and a rating-based leaderboard. Offline bot play remains available when the user has no internet connection.

**Feature Priority list**

P0 \- Core

- User registration and login (JWT auth)
- Basic game board \- carry over existing Tacfinity logic to React
- Real-time 1v1 gameplay via Socket.io
- Custom rooms with shareable code
- PostgreSQL schema setup (User, Game, GamePlayer, Move)

P1 \- Required Features

- Matchmaking queue (in-memory, auto-match by availability)
- PWA setup \- service worker \+ offline detection
- Offline bot play (port existing AI engine)
- Cron job: abandoned game cleanup
- ELO rating updates after each game
- Responsive mobile layout
- Lighthouse performance tuning (to hit 90+)

P2 \- Polish

- Reconnection handling on disconnect
- Game result screen with rating change shown
- User profile page with match history
- Leaderboard page
- Cron job: rating decay

P3 \- Nice to Have

- Spectator mode
- Room settings customization in lobby (board size, win condition before match starts)
- Rematch button
- Friends list or invite by username

**Tech Stack**  
Frontend  
React \+ TypeScript  
Vite  
TailwindCSS

Backend  
Node.js \+ Express  
Socket.io (WebSockets)  
PostgreSQL  
node-cron (cron jobs)

**Data Model**

![][image1]

**User (id, username, email, password_hash, rating, created_at, last_active_at)**

Stores account information and the player's current ELO rating. last_active_at is updated after every game and is used by the rating decay cron job to identify inactive players. Rating defaults to 1000 on registration.

**Room (id, code, host_id, board_size, win_condition, type, status, created_at)**

Represents the lobby before and between games. It holds the game settings so that all games played inside it share the same configuration. host_id points to the user who created the room and controls starting the game. Status tracks whether the room is still accepting players, currently in a game, or finished.

**RoomPlayer (id, room_id, user_id (enum: player | spectator), role, joined_at)**

Tracks all users currently inside a room lobby. This is separate from GamePlayer because players exist in a room before a game starts. It answers the question "who is in this lobby right now" independently of whether a game is ongoing. This also paves the way for adding spectators to view matches in the future.

**Game (id, room_id, status, winner_id, moves (text nullable), started_at, ended_at)**

Represents a single match played inside a room. A room can have multiple games, i.e., if players rematch. It records the outcome via winner_id, which is nullable to account for draws or abandoned games. The moves column stores the entire game sequence as a TGN (Tacfinity Game Notation) string. TGN is our own compact notation where each move is represented as a symbol: row, col, separated by pipes, for example X:0,0|O:1,1|X:0,2. This column is null while the game is ongoing since moves are held in server memory during play, and is written in a single DB update the moment the game ends. This means the entire game, regardless of board size, costs exactly one database write. The board can always be reconstructed for replay or reconnection by parsing the TGN string and replaying moves in order onto an empty grid.

**GamePlayer (id, game_id, user_id, symbol, rating_before, rating_after (nullable), joined_at)**

Links users to a specific game and records their role in it. symbol stores whether they played as X or O. Critically, rating_before and rating_after are snapshots of the player's ELO at the time of the game. This means match history is always accurate, even if the player's current rating changes later.

**Tacfinity Game Notation (TGN)**

TGN is a custom notation format designed specifically for Tacfinity to represent a complete game sequence as a single compact string. It is inspired by chess.com's use of PGN (Portable Game Notation), which also stores games as a sequence of moves rather than board snapshots.

**Format**

Each move is represented as symbol:row,col and moves are separated by a pipe character |. The string is written left to right in the order the moves were played.

```
X:0,0|O:1,1|X:0,1|O:2,2|X:0,2
```

A full 3x3 game that results in X winning would look like this, reconstructed visually:

```
X  X  X   ← win
.  O  .
.  .  O
```

For larger boards, the format stays the same. A move on a 20x20 board is just:

```
X:14,7
```

No changes to the format are needed regardless of board size.

**Why TGN**

The alternative was a separate Move table with one row per move. On a 20x20 board that is up to 400 rows per game. TGN eliminates that. The whole game is one text column on the Game table, written in a single database update the moment the game ends. During an active game, moves are held in server memory as an array and only serialized into TGN on game completion.

**How TGN is used in the app**

1. Persistence \- when a game ends the server maps the in-memory moves array into a TGN string and writes it to the moves column on the Game row in one update query.
2. Replay \- to replay a finished game, the client fetches the Game row, parses the TGN string by splitting on |, and replays each move onto an empty grid in order.
3. Reconnection \- if a player disconnects mid-game, the server still has the moves in memory. It serializes the current state into TGN, sends it to the reconnecting client via Socket.io, and the client reconstructs the board from it.
4. Websocket transmission \- during live play, individual moves are transmitted over Socket.io as compact TGN tokens rather than verbose JSON objects. For example:

```
// instead of this
{ "row": 0, "col": 2, "symbol": "X", "move_number": 3 }

// we send this
"X:0,2"
```

**Features**

Auth

- Register and login with email and password
- JWT-based sessions
- Protected routes on the frontend

Online Multiplayer

- Matchmaking \- join a queue and get auto-matched with another player of similar rating, the queue is held in memory
- Custom Rooms \- create a room with a shareable 6-char code, set board size and win condition, and invite a friend
- Real-time gameplay via WebSockets (Socket.io)
- Reconnection handling if a player drops \- board state reconstructed from moves

Leaderboard & Rating

- ELO-based rating system, default 800
- Rating snapshot stored per game in GamePlayer, so history is always accurate
- Global leaderboard page showing top players
- Casual vs ranked room types \- only the ranked affects ELO

Offline Mode (PWA)

- Service worker caches the app shell
- When offline, multiplayer is disabled, and the user is prompted to play against the bot instead
- All three AI difficulty levels remain available offline

Cron Jobs

- Rating decay \- players who haven't played for a certain amount of time lose a small amount of rating to keep the leaderboard active
- Abandoned game cleanup
- Weekly leaderboard reset
- Matchmaking queue timeout

**Planned API Endpoints**

| Method | Endpoint          | Description                   |
| :----- | :---------------- | :---------------------------- |
| POST   | api/auth/register | Create acc                    |
| POST   | api/auth/login    | login                         |
| GET    | api/users/:id     | Get user profile              |
| GET    | api/leaderboard   | Get top players               |
| POST   | api/rooms         | create room                   |
| GET    | api/rooms/:code   | Get room info                 |
| GET    | api/games/:id     | Get the game result and moves |

Real-time events via Socket.io will handle matchmaking, moves, and game state, not REST.

**WebSocket Events**

| event         | direction        | desc                      |
| :------------ | :--------------- | :------------------------ |
| queue:join    | Client to server | Enter matchmaking queue   |
| queue:matched | Server to client | Match found               |
| room:join     | Client to server | Join a custom room        |
| game:move     | Client to server | Player submits move       |
| game:update   | Server to client | Broadcast new board state |
| game:end      | Server to client | Game over                 |
